books_read: What We Owe The Future — William MacAskill
On the Nature of Things — Lucretius
The Last Days of Socrates — Plato
On the Ends of Good and Evil — Cicero
On the Move: A Life — Oliver Sacks
One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich — Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn
Mituri, vise și mistere — Mircea Eliade
Ispita de a exista — Emil Cioran
Concerning the Spiritual in Art — Wassily Kandinsky
The Ethics of Ambiguity — Simone de Beauvoir
Groundwork of the Metaphysic of Morals — Immanuel Kant
The Myth of Sisyphus — Albert Camus
The Power of Myth — Joseph Campbell
Secondhand Time: The Last of the Soviets — Svetlana Alexievich
The Kingdom of God Is within You — Leo Tolstoy
La rebelión de las masas — José Ortega y Gasset
Man's Search for Meaning — Viktor Frankl
A Farewell to Arms — Ernest Hemingway
The Sun Also Rises — Ernest Hemingway
Modern Man in Search of a Soul — Carl Jung
Thinking, Fast and Slow — Daniel Kahneman
Meditations on First Philosophy — René Descartes
An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding — David Hume
What I Talk About When I Talk About Running — Haruki Murakami
La senda del perdedor — Charles Bukowski
The Book of Disquiet — Fernando Pessoa
The Denial of Death — Ernest Becker
The Great Mental Models Vol 1–4 — Shane Parrish
The Life You Can Save — Peter Singer
The Longing for Less — Kyle Chayka
films: Blue Is the Warmest Color — Abdellatif Kechiche
The Wolf of Wall Street — Martin Scorsese
The Grand Budapest Hotel — Wes Anderson
Mad Max: Fury Road — George Miller
A Cure for Wellness — Gore Verbinski
The Secret of the Grain — Abdellatif Kechiche
The Worst Person in the World — Joachim Trier
The Hand of God — Paolo Sorrentino
Once Upon a Time in Anatolia — Nuri Bilge Ceylan
I Lost My Body — Jérémy Clapin
Notes on Blindness — Peter Middleton, James spinney
There Will Be Blood — Paul Thomas Anderson
Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri — Martin McDonagh
The Killing of a Sacred Deer — Yorgos Lanthimos
Never Let Me Go — Mark Romanek
The Book of Eli — Albert Hughes, Allen Hughes
The Tree of Life — Terrence Malick
music: Arvo Pärt — tintinnabuli period, particularly Spiegel im Spiegel and Fratres
Johann Sebastian Bach — Goldberg Variations, cello suites
Max Richter — Sleep, On the Nature of Daylight
Brian Eno — ambient works, Thursday Afternoon
Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds — later period
interests: Forests, moss, lichen, bark, and the aesthetics of slow biological processes
Autumn as a season of disclosure and visible transformation
Fermentation — sauerkraut, kimchi, kvass, pickles — as controlled decomposition and daily ritual
Veganism as a philosophical and ethical position, not a dietary identity
Walking as contemplative practice and a form of phenomenological attention
Romanian cultural identity and literature — the specific sensibility of Cărtărescu, Cioran, Eliade as a national thread
Spanish language and culture — Mediterranean life, food, architecture, the rhythm of Southern European time
Phenomenology — Husserl, Merleau-Ponty, Heidegger — embodied perception, lived time, being-in-the-world
Existentialism — the encounter with meaninglessness, radical freedom, and the problem of authentic living
Stoic philosophy as a practice of attention to what endures, not as productivity framework
Death, finitude, and mortality as genuine intellectual and lived concerns — not morbidly but rigorously
Exile and displacement literature — writers producing work outside their native language or nation
Dream logic, surreal and oneiric narrative, fiction that operates like memory rather than plot
Slow cinema — Tarkovsky, Malick, Bresson, Bergman, Akerman — duration, patience, the long take as argument
Complexity and emergence — how simple local rules produce large irreducible outcomes
Cognitive science and neuroscience — predictive processing, embodied cognition, attention, interoception
Systems thinking and fragility — second-order effects, hidden interdependencies, the punishing of certainty
Fungi, mycorrhizal networks, and the distributed intelligence of biological systems without centres
Minimalist design — restraint, coherence through subtraction, silence as compositional element
Silence and solitude as positive states — not absence but a different quality of attention
Mental models and the structural limits of rationality — Kahneman, Haidt, motivated reasoning, bias
Ethics — effective altruism in tension with existential and phenomenological approaches to the good
Sacred language and ritual without doctrine — the form of belief persisting after its content changes
Liminal spaces, thresholds, and transitional architecture — the personality of in-between places
Taoism and Confucianism as practiced attention and relational ethics rather than institutional doctrine
Decay, rot, and transformation — fallen wood, oxidation, the second life of abandoned things
New Zealand landscape — native forest, geological silence, the experience of extreme geographic distance
The relationship between images and memory — how pictures carry emotional form across centuries

History of the ancient and pre-medieval world — Neolithic through late antiquity, focused on Europe and the Middle East: megalithic cultures, the Bronze Age collapse, pre-Socratic Greece, the Roman Republic and Empire, early Christianity and its pagan substrates, the late Roman world and its fragmentation
Ancient Mediterranean geography — the physical world of Greece, Anatolia, the Levant, North Africa, and the Italian peninsula as lived and contested space
Prehistoric Europe — Neolithic settlement patterns, megalithic monuments, the spread of agriculture, the Bronze and Iron Age transitions
The relationship between landscape and civilisation — how geography shaped the Greek polis, Roman expansion, and the trade routes of the ancient Near East
Mythology and ancient religion — Greek, Roman, Near Eastern, and Celtic mythological systems as windows into how early cultures organised the cosmos
The archaeology of everyday life in antiquity — domestic space, food, ritual objects, burial practice — not just military and political history
Vegan food culture — fermentation, whole ingredients, the aesthetics of restraint in cooking
Running and movement as meditative and cognitive practice — the mind in motion, not sport
Photography and visual attention — the ethics of looking, the still image as frozen duration
The phenomenology of inhabited space — Bachelard, the emotional life of rooms, domestic geography
Impermanence and transience as aesthetic and philosophical categories — wabi-sabi, memento mori, the beauty of things in the process of ending
Cosmology and the structure of the universe — the scale of the observable universe, dark matter and dark energy, the cosmic microwave background, the large-scale structure of spacetime
Stellar evolution and the life cycles of stars — stellar nucleosynthesis, supernovae, neutron stars and black holes, the origin of heavy elements in stellar death
The pre-Socratic cosmological tradition — Anaximander, Heraclitus, Empedocles — early Greek attempts to account for the origin and structure of the cosmos before Plato's metaphysical turn
Cosmological philosophy — the anthropic principle, the Fermi paradox, the question of cosmic fine-tuning, and what the scale of the universe implies for human meaning and ethics

The history of astronomy — from Babylonian star catalogues through Copernicus, Galileo, and Kepler to modern observational astrophysics; how cosmological models have shaped and been shaped by philosophical worldviews
Acoustic ecology and the phenomenology of soundscapes — R. Murray Schafer, the quality of silence, the distinction between natural and industrial sound environments
The creative process as subject — what artists, scientists, and writers reveal in diaries, notebooks, and letters about how they actually work; the conditions that open or close original thought
Wonder as a philosophical and moral category — the cultivation of astonishment as resistance to habituation and cynicism; curiosity as an ethical stance toward the world
The ethics of attention — Simone Weil's framing of attention as the highest form of love; looking fully at another as the ground of moral life; presence as a moral act
Letters and private correspondence as intellectual form — the uncensored voice revealed through exchange; epistolary intimacy as a record of the inner life of ideas
Poetry as epistemology — verse as a way of knowing that argument cannot reach; the compressed image that carries what propositions cannot
The inner lives of great thinkers — biography as intellectual history; the private doubts, contradictions, and vulnerabilities behind finished public works and systems
Nature writing and the literature of the wild — human consciousness encountering the non-human world; ecology as a philosophical discipline, not merely a scientific one

The intersection of art and science as a unified mode of inquiry — the poet who understands thermodynamics, the physicist who writes verse; against the fragmentation of knowledge into separate languages
Children's illustrated books as compressed philosophy — Saint-Exupéry, Sendak, A.A. Milne — picture books capable of genuine philosophical seriousness
Grief, mourning, and the literature of loss — how humans use language to approach what resists direct speech; elegies, consolations, the long work of coming to terms
Marginalia and the reader's trace — annotations left in books, the intellectual life of the page as palimpsest, the relationship between reading and writing in the margin
The diary and journal as literary form — not memoir but the live record of a consciousness in process; the journal as a form of thinking rather than reporting
Light — its physics, its phenomenology, its role in painting; the aesthetics of dawn, dusk, the quality of winter light as a subject of both science and art
Color theory and the psychology of color — Goethe's color theory, Josef Albers's interactions of color; the emotional life of hue and the history of pigment
Trees and old growth as subjects of philosophical attention — longevity, rootedness, mycorrhizal kinship; the tree as a figure for time and patience
Birds and migration — the seasonal return as philosophical event; the attention required to observe; the literature of ornithology from Gilbert White onward
The ocean as philosophical space — depth as metaphor for the unconscious, tidal rhythm, the sublime of scale, the strangeness of what lives below the surface
Gardens and cultivation as metaphor — tending as a form of thought; the garden as the mind externalised; cultivation as counterpoint to extraction
Maps and cartography as metaphor — the act of representing space as an act of interpretation; the gap between the map and the territory as both philosophical problem and lived experience
Urban walking and psychogeography — the flâneur tradition, the Situationist dérive, the city as text read at walking pace; place as a form of meaning
The philosophy of friendship — Montaigne, Cicero — the specific quality of intellectual companionship across time and correspondence; friendship as a form of knowing
Aging, time's passage, and late-period creativity — what artists and thinkers produce in old age; the wisdom traditions around growing old without diminishing
The relationship between constraint and creativity — how formal limits, material resistance, and scarcity generate rather than prevent original form
Cross-cultural translation and the untranslatable — what is lost and found when a text moves between languages; the specific texture that resists conversion
Ekphrasis and the art of writing about art — the poem in front of the painting; the verbal response to the visual; what one medium can say about another
Sleep, dreams, and the unconscious as literary and philosophical territory — the tradition of what happens when the rational mind yields, beyond psychoanalysis
The history of the book and the practice of reading — manuscripts, printing, the materiality of preserved thought; libraries and archives as cathedrals of accumulated attention
Deep time and evolutionary timescales — the effect of contemplating scales that dwarf the human; geological, evolutionary, and cosmological time as philosophical contexts
First principles reasoning — stripping away convention and inherited assumption to rebuild understanding from the ground up; Socratic method as a living practice
Second-order thinking — the consequences of consequences; what happens after the obvious next thing; tracing causality beyond the first visible effect
Circle of competence — mapping the real boundary between what you actually know and what you merely believe; the discipline of epistemic honesty about your own limits
Inversion — approaching problems backwards; imagining failure before imagining success; what would guarantee the worst outcome as a guide to avoiding it
The latticework of mental models across disciplines — using physics, biology, economics, and history as lenses on the same problem; intellectual arbitrage across fields
Probabilistic thinking and Bayesian belief-updating — holding conclusions with calibrated uncertainty; revising beliefs in proportion to evidence rather than defending them against it
Compounding in knowledge and relationships — the disproportionate long-run returns of patient accumulation; the slow power of consistency over time
The difference between intelligence and wisdom — high IQ vs. good judgment; the gap between knowing and deciding well; intelligence that doesn't translate into good decisions
Deliberate practice and the actual mechanics of expertise — what separates world-class from merely competent; practice that targets weakness vs. naive repetition of strength
Skin in the game and accountability structures — why advice changes when the advisor bears the consequences; the moral weight of having real stakes
Managing energy and attention rather than time — the human as a biological system with limits on cognitive output; recovery and renewal as part of the performance equation
Self-deception and motivated reasoning — the multiple ways we protect prior beliefs from evidence; the inner lawyer that constructs justifications rather than seeking truth
Reading as a craft — annotating, re-reading, going to primary sources; the difference between reading for information and reading for understanding and transformation
The long game — delayed gratification, patient compounding, resisting the tyranny of short-term thinking; building things that take longer than a news cycle to matter
Decision-making under uncertainty — frameworks for choosing when information is incomplete; separating the quality of the process from the quality of the outcome
Signal vs. noise — the discipline of filtering; what actually matters vs. what is merely loud; the attention economy as a force that systematically rewards noise
The quality of questions as a determinant of the quality of thinking — how the frame shapes what answers are visible; asking different questions rather than better answers to the same ones
Writing as a tool for thinking — the essay as a form of working out what you believe; the act of writing as a way of discovering what you actually think
trusted_publications: The Marginalian — marginalian.org — Maria Popova's long-form essays at the intersection of art, science, philosophy, and literature; the benchmark for lyrical, rigorously sourced intellectual writing
Farnam Street — fs.blog — Shane Parrish on mental models, decision-making, and the craft of clear thinking; the benchmark for applied wisdom without productivity framing
Aeon — aeon.co — philosophy, science, and culture written for a serious general reader; rigorous but accessible long-form that treats ideas with full seriousness
The Paris Review — interviews with writers about craft and the inner life of creative work; the primary record of how writers actually think about what they do
Noema Magazine — technology, politics, and culture examined at philosophical depth; ideas about the future that take history and ethics seriously
Nautilus — science writing that earns its ideas through narrative rather than bullet points; the place where a biology finding becomes a meditation on time
Literary Hub — the literary internet at its most considered; book culture, essays, and the writer's life without celebrity packaging
The New York Review of Books — long-form essay-reviews that treat books as occasions for serious thought; the essay that runs to ten thousand words because it needs to
Cabinet Magazine — art, ideas, and the overlooked corners of intellectual and cultural history; the small-format publication that thinks at large scale
The Hedgehog Review — cultural criticism grounded in social theory and intellectual history; serious thinking about what holds a culture together or apart
On Being — Krista Tippett's long-form conversations at the intersection of spirituality, science, ethics, and meaning-making; depth without dogma
The Point Magazine — philosophy brought back to the question of how to live; ideas tested against experience rather than reduced to argument
Lapham's Quarterly — history and literature organised around a single theme per issue; the past speaking directly to the present through primary sources
The Sun Magazine — personal essays and interviews that take suffering and beauty with equal seriousness; emotional honesty without sentimentality
avoid: Hustle culture and productivity framing — the reduction of wisdom traditions to output, efficiency, or personal optimisation; any piece that treats philosophy as a performance-enhancing tool
Motivational content that reframes difficulty as a branding opportunity or suffering as fuel for success
Tech solutionism and startup optimism — the assumption that technological progress is straightforwardly good, inevitable, or the most interesting thing happening
Philosophy treated as self-help — ideas stripped of their difficulty and packaged as actionable takeaways or morning routines
Celebrity culture, entertainment news, and anything whose primary context is fame, popularity, or the attention economy
Listicles, hot takes, and engagement-bait — content structured around algorithmic performance rather than genuine thought
Forced resolution — pieces that tie tension into a lesson, a moral, or an encouraging conclusion; the essay that tells the reader what to feel at the end
Pop psychology dressed as insight — the reduction of complex human behaviour to simple, flattering explanations that confirm what readers already believe
Naive optimism and forced positivity — the insistence that everything is improving, that effort always pays off, that difficulty is always temporary
Moral grandstanding without philosophical grounding — positions performed for an audience rather than worked through with genuine uncertainty
Content that assumes the reader needs to be persuaded toward obvious conclusions or nudged toward virtuous behaviour
Jargon-heavy academic writing that uses complexity to signal belonging rather than to clarify what is genuinely difficult
what_resonates_in_writing: Unexpected connections between distant fields or traditions — the moment two ideas from opposite ends of the map illuminate each other without forcing
The concrete image that carries a large abstract idea — not illustration appended to argument, but the image that is the thought
The personal made universal without being confessional — the writer's experience used as a lens, not as the subject
Earned emotion — feeling that arrives through accumulation and precision, not through assertion or manufactured sentiment
Sentences that reward re-reading — where the second pass reveals a layer the first pass moved through too quickly
The moment a piece turns — where the essay's real question becomes visible, often arriving later than expected
Compression: the paragraph that does the work of a chapter without appearing to hurry or summarise
Genuine intellectual humility — writing that shows its uncertainty rather than performing confidence; the thinker who admits what they do not know
Ideas that reframe something familiar so it cannot be unseen — the changed perception that persists long after the reading is over
Writing that ends in tension rather than resolution — the reader left with something open, unfinished, still live
Prose that moves between registers — the lyrical and the analytical inhabiting the same paragraph without violence to either
Specificity over abstraction — the named place, the exact year, the particular species, the actual sentence rather than the general category
The lesser-known source over the canonical one — the passage that does the same work as the famous one but arrives as a discovery rather than a confirmation
values: Depth over novelty — the preference for going further into one thing rather than skimming many; the refusal of the scroll as a mode of thinking
Attention as a moral act — the belief that how we pay attention shapes who we become and what we owe to others and to the world
The examined life as a commitment, not a pose — genuine reckoning with how to live, held alongside the knowledge that it cannot be fully resolved
Ethical seriousness without dogmatism — caring deeply about how to live without claiming certainty about the answers; the open question as a permanent condition
The unity of aesthetics and ethics — beauty and goodness as related, not separate, concerns; how something is made matters alongside what it says
Intellectual honesty — the willingness to hold contradiction, revise positions under pressure, and follow arguments to uncomfortable places
The long game — patient accumulation over quick results; the compounding value of consistent, quiet attention sustained across years
Slowness as a form of respect — to ideas, to people, to the non-human world; the refusal of speed as a value in itself
The moral weight of the non-human world — the seriousness of other species, ecosystems, and the biological fabric as genuine ethical concerns, not merely aesthetic ones
Solitude and silence as positive states — not absence but a different quality of presence; the generative rather than merely recuperative value of being alone and quiet
recurring_motifs: Forests, moss, bark, lichen — biological patience; slow time made visible in the texture of living surfaces; the non-human life that predates and will outlast
Autumn — disclosure and visible transformation; the season when things show what they are in the process of ending; beauty inseparable from loss
Fermentation — controlled transformation; time made visible in matter; the generative potential of managed decay; patience as method
Decay, rot, and the second life of things — fallen wood, oxidation, the persistence and transformation of form; ending as a kind of beginning
Walking — the body in motion as a mode of thought; pace as an epistemic condition; what becomes available to the mind when the legs are moving
Light and its qualities — direction, season, the difference between morning and late afternoon; light as mood and argument; the way winter light falls differently than summer
Thresholds and liminal spaces — the in-between; transitional architecture and states; doorways, shorelines, dusk; the personality of not-yet and no-longer
Silence — as positive presence rather than absence; the texture of genuine quiet; what silence makes possible that noise forecloses
The archive and the preserved — books, marginalia, the accumulated record; what outlasts its maker; the strange persistence of thought across centuries
The small and overlooked — fungi, lichen, marginalia, the footnote — the not-headline as a site of genuine significance; the refusal of scale as the measure of importance
Deep time — the geological, evolutionary, and cosmic scales that dwarf the human and recontextualise the urgent; the perspective that makes the present strange
Roots and rootedness — literal (trees, underground networks, mycorrhizal webs) and cultural (Romanian identity, Mediterranean life) — the question of what holds and what it costs to be held
podcasts_and_audio: The Knowledge Project — Shane Parrish in long-form conversation with thinkers and practitioners on decision-making, mental models, and the craft of living and thinking well
On Being — Krista Tippett's conversations at the intersection of spirituality, science, ethics, and the meaning-making traditions; depth without institutional religion
Philosophize This! — Stephen West's accessible but rigorous solo surveys of the philosophical tradition, from the pre-Socratics to the twentieth century
Sean Carroll's Mindscape — physics, philosophy, and the foundations of reality; hard science held alongside its philosophical implications without reducing either
In Our Time — Melvyn Bragg's BBC programme exploring the history of ideas across science, philosophy, culture, and history; the expert conversation format at its best
History of Philosophy Without Any Gaps — Peter Adamson's comprehensive, precise, and warm survey of the entire philosophical tradition including non-Western philosophy
